learn.title: Grid Balancing Supply & Demand
learn.intro: Real-time grid balancing ensures reliable electricity delivery amid fluctuating supply (e.g., solar/wind) and demand. This dynamic process is critical for integrating renewables and maintaining grid stability.
learn.c1_text: Grid frequency (50Hz in India) must stay stable. When demand spikes, turbines ramp up generation instantly. For example, a 100 MW coal plant can adjust output in seconds to match load changes.
learn.c2_text: Reserve capacity (e.g., 15% of India's grid capacity) acts as a safety net. These backup power plants respond within minutes to unexpected outages or surges, ensuring uninterrupted supply.
learn.c3_text: India's grid faces unique challenges due to its size and rapid renewable adoption. The country's grid operator, POSOCO, manages over 450 GW of diverse generation, requiring advanced forecasting and real-time controls.
learn.stat2: Solar/wind contribute 20% of India's grid capacity (2024)
learn.stat3: Grid frequency deviations must stay within ±0.5 Hz (standard)
learn.stat4: India's renewable energy target: 500 GW by 2030

learn.title: Geothermal Energy Basics
learn.intro: Geothermal energy taps Earth's internal heat for electricity and direct heating. It provides baseload power with minimal emissions, making it a reliable renewable option.
learn.c1_text: Earth's core reaches 5,000°C. Geothermal plants use steam or hot water from underground reservoirs. For example, Iceland meets 40% of its electricity needs via geothermal.
learn.c2_text: Dry steam plants (e.g., California's Geysers) use steam directly to spin turbines. Binary cycle plants transfer heat to a secondary fluid, enabling use in areas with lower-temperature resources.
learn.c3_text: India has significant geothermal potential, with estimated 10,000 MW of recoverable energy. The Parvati Geothermal Project in Maharashtra aims to harness 10 MW, aligning with national clean energy goals.

learn.title: Direct Air Carbon Capture
learn.intro: Direct air capture (DAC) removes CO₂ from the atmosphere using renewable energy. This technology is vital for achieving net-zero emissions but faces scalability and cost challenges.
learn.c1_text: DAC uses chemical sorbents to absorb CO₂ from air. For example, Climeworks' plants in Iceland capture 4,000 tons annually, storing it underground via mineralization.
learn.c2_text: Renewables power DAC to ensure net-negative emissions. A 1 MW DAC plant requires ~100 MW of renewable energy, highlighting the need for grid-scale solar/wind integration.
learn.c3_text: India's National Carbon Market and PM-KISAN scheme support carbon capture. Projects like the Carbon Capture Utilization (CCU) initiative aim to reduce emissions from industries while boosting rural economies.
learn.stat3: India's carbon market target: $2 billion by 2030

learn.title: Solar Panel Types Compared
learn.intro: Solar panels vary in efficiency, cost, and application. Monocrystalline panels are efficient but expensive, while polycrystalline and thin-film offer trade-offs for different use cases.
learn.c1_text: Monocrystalline panels (20-22% efficiency) use single-crystal silicon. They perform better in low light, making them ideal for rooftops. India's solar parks use these for high-output systems.
learn.c2_text: Polycrystalline (15-17% efficiency) is cheaper but less efficient. Thin-film (10-13%) is flexible and lightweight, suitable for large-scale projects. India's Bhadla Solar Park uses thin-film for cost-effective deployment.
learn.c3_text: India's solar capacity reached 70 GW (2024), with 60% from utility-scale projects. Monocrystalline dominates rooftop installations, while thin-film is favored for large solar farms due to lower costs.

learn.title: Solar PV Electricity Generation
learn.intro: Solar PV panels convert sunlight into electricity via the photoelectric effect. This process is clean, scalable, and central to global renewable energy strategies.
learn.c1_text: Photons from sunlight hit silicon cells, knocking electrons loose. This creates a current. A 1 m² panel can generate 150-200 W under peak sunlight, powering a home for hours.
learn.c2_text: DC electricity from panels is converted to AC by inverters. Modern inverters (e.g., SMA) achieve 98% efficiency, ensuring minimal energy loss during conversion.
learn.c3_text: India's solar PV capacity grew 20% annually, with 10 million rooftop systems installed. The government's PM Surya Ghar scheme aims to install 100 GW of rooftop solar by 2027.
learn.stat4: PM Surya Ghar target: 100 GW by 2027

learn.title: Pumped Hydro Storage
learn.intro: Pumped hydro storage is the world's largest battery, using water to store energy. It balances grid fluctuations and supports renewable integration by storing excess energy for later use.
learn.c1_text: Excess energy pumps water uphill to a reservoir. When needed, water flows down through turbines, generating electricity. For example, the Bath County Pumped Storage Station in the U.S. stores 3 GW.
learn.c2_text: Pumped hydro has 70-85% round-trip efficiency. It can store 100+ GWh, making it ideal for grid-scale storage. India's Rishi Ganga project aims to add 1 GW of storage capacity.
learn.c3_text: India has 10 potential sites for pumped hydro, with 50 GW of theoretical capacity. The Central Electricity Authority (CEA) prioritizes these projects to support renewable energy integration and grid stability.
learn.stat3: India's Rishi Ganga target: 1 GW by 2025
learn.stat4: CEA: 50 GW potential for pumped hydro
